Most recruiters don’t need another sourcing tool.

They need smarter prompts.

ChatGPT won’t fill your cleared roles for you.
But it can cut hours off your week.

If you’re still only using it for job posts and “write an email,” you’re leaving a lot on the table.

Here are recruiter-first prompts you can steal:

1. Sourcing strings
↳Have it translate reqs into Boolean/X-ray for your stack
↳Ex: "You are a senior technical sourcer. Create 3 advanced Boolean and X-ray strings to find TS/SCI cleared cloud engineers on LinkedIn + ClearanceJobs based on this JD: [paste]."

2. Intake meetings
↳Prep sharper questions for niche GovCon/AI/cyber roles
↳Ex: "Turn this job description + these notes into 10 intake questions that clarify must-have skills, clearance level, and customer environment."

3. Translating jargon
↳Turn program-speak into actual skills to source on
↳Ex: "Translate this hiring manager jargon into a skills/tech stack list I can search for: [paste email or SOW]."

4. Outreach personalization
↳Layer in mission, tech stack, and clearance fit fast
↳Ex: "Write 5 short InMail templates for cleared AI engineers that reference their project keywords and this program’s mission: [details]. Keep it under 120 words."

5. Screening questions
↳Design quick, high-signal screens for complex roles
↳Ex: "Create 7 screening questions to validate zero trust experience and clearance status for this cyber role. Include ideal answer cues."

6. Candidate rediscovery
↳Mine your ATS for hidden matches
↳Ex: "Based on this new JD, write filters and tags I should use to rediscover past candidates in my ATS who are 70%+ relevant."

7. JD clean-up
↳Make dense SOWs readable and candidate-friendly
↳Ex: "Turn this SOW into a clear job description for senior cloud engineers. Plain language, highlight impact, keep clearance/compliance accurate."

8. Compliance + clearance checks
↳Stress-test your messages and JDs before they go out
↳Ex: "Review this outreach + JD for a TS/SCI role. Flag anything that might be non-compliant or unclear about clearance/onsite/contract terms."

9. Market intel fast pass
↳Get a rough map of talent pools before you promise timelines
↳Ex: "Estimate where I’m most likely to find TS/SCI cleared Kubernetes engineers (by region and platform) and suggest 5 sourcing angles."

10. Reusable recruiting templates
↳Turn what works into a system you can copy/paste
↳Ex: "Based on these 3 prompts that worked well, create a reusable prompt template I can use for any new cleared technical req."

The gap in recruiting isn’t just talent.
It’s how we’re using the tools sitting right in front of us.

What’s one AI prompt or use-case that’s actually helped you close a role faster? Share it below so other recruiters can steal it.

Celebrity endorsements don’t sell trust. People do.

In my 8+ years in digital marketing, I’ve seen countless brands blow their entire budget on "aspiration"—hiring the biggest stars to create a dream. But if you’re selling a high-anxiety product (like gold, insurance, or even high-ticket SaaS), aspiration isn’t what the customer needs.

They need to know they aren't being cheated.

I’ve been analyzing Lalithaa Jewellery’s "Trust-First" framework, and it’s a masterclass in psychological marketing. While their competitors hired Bollywood icons, the founder put his own face on the billboards. No airbrushing, no script—just a "bald guy" telling you that money doesn't come easily.

The result? A ₹13,000+ crore empire built on the back of removing doubt rather than creating desire.

Here are 3 lessons I’ve learned about building authority in "high-stakes" categories:

The "Challenge" Beats the "Claim" Lalithaa told customers to take photos of their prices and compare them with competitors. When you stop saying "Trust me" and start saying "Test me," you win.

Education is the Ultimate Sales Tool
Instead of flashy ads, they created content on how to calculate "wastage" and avoid being overcharged. If you are the one teaching the customer how to buy wisely, you are the one they will eventually buy from.

Authenticity Trumps Perfection
In a world of AI-generated polish, a founder who speaks the customer's language (literally and figuratively) creates a bond that no celebrity contract can buy.

Marketing isn't always about making things look pretty. Sometimes, it’s about making them feel safe.

Which do you think is more powerful for a brand today: A-list celebrity backing or a visible, transparent founder?

Executive Analysis: Moving from "Rent-a-Brain" to "Asset-Backed Services"

​The traditional service-based model is under valuation pressure. Historically, firms were valued on a multiple of EBITDA, driven by billable hours. Today, the market is discounting firms that rely solely on human labor, fearing they will be cannibalized by automated competitors.

​To survive the "Efficiency Trap," the C-Suite must transition the firm from a Service Provider to an Insights Factory.

​1. The Death of the Billable Hour
​If your revenue is tied to time, AI is your enemy. As AI compresses a 10-hour task into 10 seconds, your revenue collapses under a traditional billing model.
​The Shift: Executives must pivot to Value-Based Pricing. The Strategy: Charge for the outcome (e.g., a successful audit, a secured network, a completed contract extraction) rather than the effort. This allows the firm to capture 100% of the margin created by AI-driven speed.

​2. Manufacturing "Institutional Alpha"
​The most valuable asset in a service firm is the collective experience of its senior partners. Usually, this is "volatile" asset it leaves the building every evening.
​The Solution: Use Neural Indexing to capture every decision, every strategic pivot, and every successful project architecture into a proprietary "Firm-Specific Model."
​The Result: You aren't just selling a consultant's time; you are selling access to the firm’s Collective Intelligence. This creates a "sticky" moat that a generic LLM cannot replicate.

​3. The "Zero-Trust" Quality Shield
​As AI-generated content floods the market, "Quality" becomes the rarest commodity. The C-Suite’s new mandate is Total Verifiability.
​The Mechanism: Implementing AI-driven "Double-Loop Auditing." One AI system executes the service, while a second, independent "Agentic Auditor" verifies the output against regulatory and firm-specific standards.
​The Value Prop: You aren't selling a report; you are selling Certainty. In a world of deepfakes and hallucinated data, "Certified Human-AI Synergy" becomes a premium brand.

​4. Capital Allocation: Investing in "Digital Twins" of Processes
​The CFO should no longer view AI as an "IT Expense" but as "Capital Expenditure (CapEx)."
​The Goal: Building a "Digital Twin" of your firm’s most profitable service lines.
​The ROI: Once a service process is digitized and agentic, it becomes an asset that can be "licensed" or "scaled" globally without adding a single desk in a physical office.

​Boardroom Checklist: The 3 Questions for 2026

​Revenue Decoupling: What percentage of our revenue is currently independent of human hours?

​Data Sovereignty: Do we own the "Fine-Tuned" logic of our AI, or are we just renting a wrapper from a Big Tech provider?

​The Talent Pivot: Are we hiring "Doers" (who AI replaces) or "Architects" (who design the AI workflows)?

Today, I wish to write about AI, lawyers and corporates.

As AI sweeps across corporate functions, a counterintuitive truth is emerging: lawyers may matter more—not less—for corporates ( even PSUs) -in the age of AI.

Much of the current discourse focuses on automation—AI drafting contracts, even aligning matters with philosophy of corporates, summarising documents, even predicting outcomes. All true. But this is precisely why the role of lawyers is evolving, not diminishing.

AI is exceptionally good at processing information.
But corporations don’t run on information alone—they run on judgment, trust, context, and accountability.

This is where lawyers step in.

Today’s in-house counsel are increasingly wearing four critical hats:

Strategic advisors to the Board — As AI enters core decision-making—pricing, hiring, supply chains—the Board faces new and often ambiguous legal risks. Lawyers are no longer just compliance gatekeepers; they are strategic enablers, helping align innovation with regulatory foresight and long-term sustainability.

Architects of governance and risk frameworks — AI introduces invisible risks: bias, opacity, data misuse, and evolving regulatory standards. Lawyers design internal AI policies and governance systems that embed responsible and accountable use of technology.

Translators between regulation, technology, and business — One of the biggest gaps in organizations today is between what technology can do and what regulation permits. Lawyers bridge this divide, ensuring that business decisions are both legally sound and commercially viable.

Custodians of ethics in an algorithm-driven world — As AI begins to influence decisions that affect lives, markets, and society, ethical considerations become central. Lawyers help institutionalize fairness, transparency, and accountability, going beyond black-letter law.

AI can suggest a clause.
It cannot judge its long-term implications.

AI can flag risks.
It cannot navigate grey zones.

AI can accelerate decisions.
But it also amplifies risk.

As routine legal work gets automated, the premium on human judgment only rises.

The future is not “AI vs Lawyers.”
It is AI + Lawyers = Better Governance, Faster Decisions, Stronger Corporates.

For corporates, the message is clear:
Don’t just invest in AI tools—invest in empowered legal teams who can guide, challenge, and govern their use.

Because when things go wrong—as they inevitably will—
no one turns to an algorithm.

They turn to a lawyer.

About 81% of recruiters said that they post "ghost jobs" (roles that either don't exist or have already been filled). Which means a meaningful chunk of the job postings you're applying to were never real opportunities to begin with.

The volume problem has overwhelmed the system. 💢 

A surge in application volume, largely fuelled by AI tools (Claude Gang!) that make it easier than ever to apply and tailor CVs at scale, means hiring teams are spending more time reviewing applications but getting less meaningful signal from each one. 

"Go to Claude, upload you Résumé and automate your job search", has become the new normal. 💥 

4 out of 5 applications are curated by AI which makes all the 4 indistinguishable. AI screening adoption by recruiters has grown substantially and hiring outcomes have gotten worse. When a job post attracts 500 applications, maintaining communication with all candidates becomes logistically challenging. You are not being ignored because you're unqualified. You're being ignored because the person who should be reading your application is buried under 499 identical applications.

🐓 What should we do?
The conventional strategy of apply-wait-get rejected-repeat has the worst results today. 
What works today is a direct warm outreach on linkedin, not a connection request but only 3 sentence message (an elevator pitch!) to the hiring manager explaining what you do and asking for a quick chat (maybe offline coffee catchup) works! 
Attending job fairs are equally unproductive, companies will direct you to apply online anyway. Job fairs are for B2B networking, generating warm leads and the sales team looking for a big contract.

🚀 Hang tight and be consisent. 

If you're an HR professional or recruiter reading this — I'd genuinely love to hear your side of the table. What does your application pile actually look like, and has AI made your job easier or just louder?

Executives don’t hire an assistant when things are calm. It’s a response to chaos. That’s it. In fact, it’s why many execs add a contract clause insisting they have an EA if they join a new company. 

It’s in anticipation of the chaos to come. 

I have never spoken to an EA who walked into a role and thought: 
🤌🏻“Wow, there are SOP’s for every process”
🤌🏻“G drive/OneDrive folders are perfectly organized” 
🤌🏻“My exec always has 2-3 hours of focus time each day”
🤌🏻“Automations/workflows are running well” 
🤌🏻“The execs calendar is a breeze”
🤌🏻“Inbox ends at zero every day”
🤌🏻“No one is ever overwhelmed” 
🤌🏻“I’m just here to maintain what’s working.” 

Ha! Could you imagine?

It’s almost a universal truth that chaos control is part of the job - it certainly was with the execs I supported and they were spread across 12 countries. 

Executives know this too. That’s why many feel the need to “clean the house before the house cleaner came.” I admire their desire and effort to pave the way for a good onboarding.

But…if you aim for perfection before hiring an assistant, you’ll never hire one. Bring them into the chaos, give them full access, prioritize your syncs, and see what magic they can do when you surrender to the partnership. 

If sales psychology tells us that people buy solutions to problems, then executives are buying a solution to chaos when they hire an EA. 

But if chaos control is where you stay, even with a stellar assistant, that’s not great. 

⭐️ Ideally, a great exec + EA partnership moves from reactivity and into proactivity.⭐️

Basically, you might hire an EA for one thing (chaos control), but find yourself with a lot more if you nurture that relationship right. 

For executive assistants, this info can become the backbone of how you show up to interviews and the job itself. 

⭐️Build a bridge from their chaos to your calm.⭐️

👉🏻Good EAs are in stage 1 which is “chaos control”. 

👉🏻Great EAs move to stage 2 which is “momentum management”. This is a place most execs can only dream of getting to because their immediate chaos feels so compressing. 

Here’s the key for executive assistants: ⭐️chaos control gets you in the door, but momentum management + chaos control keeps you there.⭐️

The Anatomy of a LinkedIn Resume Scam: A Security Researcher’s Audit.
I’m currently a 4.0 GPA student and ACM Chair, but I spent 18 years as a tattoo artist before pivoting to AI System Architecture and Red Teaming. This makes me a prime target for "Talent Scouts" looking for a specific type of vulnerability: the career transitioner.
Earlier today, I was approached by a "recruiter" claiming a client was interested in my background. Within minutes, the mask slipped.
The Red Flags (Execution Flow):
The Ghost Feedback: She claimed her client reviewed my "resume" and found it lacking. Fact: I haven’t sent her a resume yet. I’ve been working solo/contract.
The Pay-to-Play Pivot: She immediately insisted I pay $50-$80 to a "vetted expert" to optimize my resume for ATS.
The Empathy Gap: Even after explaining I live on a fixed $800 SSI income, she continued to push the paid service.
The So-What: Legitimate recruiters are paid by the employer, not the candidate. If someone asks you to pay for a "resume certification" or a "specialist review" to unlock a job lead, you aren't the candidate. You're the product.
In security, we call this a "Logic Flaw" in the hiring process. If you can't verify the edge between a "Scout" and a "Salesperson," the system is compromised.
I’m not looking for a resume writer. I’m looking for high-stakes problems to solve. If your systems are breakable, I’m the guy who finds the "Time Warp" exploit before the bad actors do.
Keep your $80. Build your own tools. Verify everything.

Law schools are making some progress in preparing new attorneys for an AI-driven legal industry, but they still have a significant way to go to fully meet the expectations of law firms and corporate legal departments.

Currently, AI education is largely confined to elective courses and secondary programs rather than standard legal education. According to a recent survey, only 6% of law students and 12% of professors report that AI is integrated into doctrinal coursework. 

Furthermore, just 5% of students and 8% of professors indicated that AI learning is a built-in part of required courses or workshops. Instead, students are encountering AI primarily through elective courses (reported by 36% of professors and 33% of students) and through specialized workshops or bootcamps.

The curriculum itself is also slow to adapt. More than half of law professors (53%) stated they have not updated their curricula in response to recent tech developments, with 31% noting they haven't even been asked or encouraged to do so by their institutions. However, there are signs of improvement, as about 48% of professors have either made requested updates or already had them in place.

This educational gap contrasts heavily with industry demand. More than half of practicing attorneys (57%) expect incoming associates to have at least a basic familiarity with generative AI upon hiring, largely because 40% of current attorneys are already using AI for tasks like legal research and drafting memos. 

Familiarizing students with AI is becoming critical, as teaching them how to appropriately use these tools can prevent future ethical issues and misuse in their legal practice.

Beyond simply knowing how to prompt AI, veteran attorneys are concerned that junior lawyers currently lack the foundational legal skills—specifically in concise contract drafting and due diligence—necessary to effectively validate AI-generated results. Without a firm grasp of these fundamentals, legal organizations face an increased risk of accidentally relying on false or flawed AI outputs.

Ultimately, while law schools are working to expand their AI offerings and fundamental training, law schools alone cannot bridge the knowledge gap. To ensure early-career attorneys are truly prepared, law firms and corporate legal teams will have to focus heavily on their own internal training programs to teach fundamental lawyering skills alongside AI workflows.
